author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c 11.8c bootstrap placement policy
summary: Implemented the bounded 11.8c BootstrapPlacementPolicy step after confirming there were no open High/Critical Claude findings. Added an opt-in BootstrapPlacementPolicy API, physical-position-backed placement counters, bundle/ordinal floor evaluation with EOM taper and minimum physical separation, checkpoint/explicit-bootstrap counter reset, and object-boundary policy bootstraps folded into the same Object journal bundle. Extended ObjectWriteSummary to expose any policy-emitted control tape files. Added tests for mid-object checkpoint rejection, bundle-floor placement, min-separation deferral, EOM taper tightening, and checkpoint counter reset. Did not mark Layer 3c complete.

author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c bulk recovery policy memory budget alignment
summary: Implemented a bounded 11.13b/addendum-v0.2 alignment step after rereading the current journal and confirming there were no open High/Critical Claude findings. Replaced the interim max_window_blocks bulk recovery policy surface with the normative max_recovery_cache_bytes, allow_windowed_recovery, and max_stripes_per_window fields; added a typed RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget error; and taught ObjectParitySource::recover_region to estimate affected stripes, account for returned Vec bytes plus recovery-window shard cache bytes, reject non-windowed over-budget plans, and derive a byte-cap-fitting window when windowed recovery is allowed. Strengthened the object-source bulk recovery test to cover ordered recovery, non-windowed budget failure, cap-derived window success, one-window budget failure, out-of-range requests, and invalid zero policy fields. This does not claim the full epoch-deduplicating planner is complete; it tightens the policy and memory gate that planner will use. Did not mark Layer 3c complete.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 8da8b665-ae40-4d33-908b-69b2edea0a79 (layer3c 11.8c bounded BootstrapPlacementPolicy + checkpoint counter reset + object-boundary bootstrap folded into the Object journal bundle). Diff scope confirmed by `git diff HEAD --stat`: crates/remanence-parity/src/lib.rs (+2 -2 re-export of BootstrapPlacementPolicy/CheckpointResult/ObjectCloseResult/ObjectWriteSummary/ParitySink/ResumeWriterSeed/SidecarTapeFile/SidecarWriteSummary) and crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs (+~500 lines). Workspace tree also carries the now-journaled eacac95b changes to crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs and crates/remanence-parity/src/error.rs that landed in the same fire as my review; those files are reviewed separately. Mapping to design §6.1 / §7.3 / §13 row 11.8c: opt-in `BootstrapPlacementPolicy` struct at crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs:441–488 (validated for non-zero floors, finite-and-in-(0,1] fractions, non-zero divisors) wired to a runtime `BootstrapPlacementState` at sink.rs:496–501 that tracks bundles-since-last, ordinals-since-last, last bootstrap start LBA, and a per-object capacity snapshot. State is updated via the new `record_physical_position` (sink.rs:2428–2430) invoked at every successful raw write/filemark outcome, so the EOM divisor and min-separation guard read the current tape cursor without an extra POSITION probe (consistent with §7.3 'physical separation' and the §7.5 reserve plumbing). `record_object_bundle_for_bootstrap_policy` (sink.rs:1881–1909) increments the staleness counters using checked arithmetic and rejects a backward protection watermark. `emit_policy_bootstrap_if_due` (sink.rs:~1911) trips on (bundles_floor || ordinals_floor) AND `bootstrap_min_separation_satisfied`, then resets counters via the trailing `write_bootstrap_with_finality` → `bootstrap_placement_state.last_bootstrap_start_lba = Some(bootstrap_start_lba)` and the outer `reset_counters()`. `active_bootstrap_policy_floors` + `bootstrap_policy_eom_divisor` pick the largest divisor whose `remaining_fraction <= threshold`, which is correct for the §7.3 example `[(0.10, 2), (0.01, 4)]` (descending fraction, ascending divisor). `set_bootstrap_placement_policy` and its clear/builder variants (sink.rs:~785–832) reject changes mid-object, mirroring §7.3 'object boundary, never mid-object' and §6.1 checkpoint preconditions; `bootstrap_placement_state.estimated_total_tape_blocks` is recomputed at `start_object_after_reserve` (sink.rs:2388–2396) from the caller's §7.5 reserve, so the EOM taper is fed by an actual capacity reserve rather than a fixed constant. Object-boundary bootstrap folding now goes through `commit_journal_map_range(Object, bundle_start, &sidecars)` (sink.rs:1825–1860): finish_object captures `bundle_start = object.tape_file_number` before the policy emit, writes the Object filemark and sidecars, calls `emit_policy_bootstrap_if_due`, then commits ONE journal bundle spanning Object + sidecars + (optional) bootstrap drawn from the live filemark map — matching §7.3 'object-boundary policy bootstraps folded into the same Object journal bundle' and the §10.6 fsync-on-commit boundary. `ObjectWriteSummary.control_tape_files_emitted` (sink.rs:359–365) surfaces the new entries to callers and is also appended in `committed_bundle()` so the writer-facing summary stays consistent. The explicit `write_bootstrap` path (sink.rs:1253–1261) commits its own Control bundle through the same map-range helper and then `bootstrap_placement_state.reset_counters()` — this is the checkpoint() reset hook required by §13 row 11.8c and exercised by `checkpoint_resets_bootstrap_placement_counters` at sink.rs:3361. checkpoint() itself (sink.rs:1270–1278) calls write_bootstrap and inherits the mid-object guard from `write_bootstrap_with_finality` (sink.rs:1286–1290 'write_bootstrap called while an object is active'), which is what `checkpoint_rejects_mid_object` at sink.rs:3201 asserts. Finding [Medium]: the §13 row 11.8c test list explicitly calls for 'checkpoint → resumable clean end; resume after checkpoint rebuilds the open epoch and a later finish() protects everything' (docs/layer3c-design.md:5336), and no such test was added in this commit — the five new tests cover mid-object rejection, bundle-floor folding, min-separation deferral, EOM-taper tightening, and counter reset, but not the resume-rebuild-finish loop that is the load-bearing claim for §13.1 (the Layer 5 checkpoint-before-UNLOAD contract that the next planned row 11.L5b depends on). Codex's entry self-discloses 'Did not mark Layer 3c complete', so this is on the follow-up agenda; surfacing it explicitly so 11.L5b is not started on top of an untested resume-after-checkpoint path. Recommended fix: add a test that calls `checkpoint()` mid-session, constructs a fresh `ParitySink` from that committed prefix via the existing `ParitySink::from_resume_seed` constructor (the §7.8 resume path), writes additional objects, calls `finish()`, and asserts (a) the rebuilt epoch covers the data written before the checkpoint and (b) the final bootstrap's projected digest protects every ordinal end-to-end. Finding [Low]: `BootstrapPlacementPolicy::validate` (crates/remanence-parity/src/sink.rs:466–488) accepts an arbitrarily-ordered `eom_taper`. `bootstrap_policy_eom_divisor` selects the `.max()` divisor over all triggered thresholds, so behavior is monotonic-in-tightening regardless of input, but docs/layer3c-design.md:2982–2986 specifies the example `[(0.10, 2), (0.01, 4)]` in descending fraction with ascending divisors, and a non-monotone input is almost certainly an akash misconfiguration that should be caught at validate-time rather than silently masked by `.max()`. Recommended fix: after the per-element checks, sort or assert that `eom_taper` is strictly descending in `remaining_fraction` AND strictly ascending in divisor, returning `ParityError::Invariant("eom_taper entries must be ordered by descending remaining_fraction with strictly increasing divisors")` otherwise. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean — initial run during the implementer's in-flight source.rs/error.rs edits showed source.rs diffs at lines 247/315/545/552 that were out of scope; verified scope-clean by `git stash` of those two files then `cargo fmt --all --check`, then `git stash pop`; after the implementer's eacac95b commit settled the tree, a final `cargo fmt --all --check` returned EXIT=0 with no diffs); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (initial run during the implementer's in-flight source.rs work failed on `crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:277` with `clippy::manual_saturating_arithmetic` — out of scope, that lint belongs to eacac95b's pre-final state; once eacac95b landed and `saturating_sub` replaced the manual pattern, the workspace clippy returned EXIT=0; the 8da8b665 scope itself was independently confirmed clean via `cargo clippy -p remanence-parity --all-targets -- -D warnings` EXIT=0); cargo test --workspace (705 passed / 0 failed / 10 ignored — up from the e825717 baseline of 700/0/8 by the five new 11.8c tests `checkpoint_rejects_mid_object`, `bootstrap_placement_policy_bundle_floor_folds_into_object_bundle`, `bootstrap_placement_policy_min_separation_defers_tripped_floor`, `bootstrap_placement_policy_eom_taper_tightens_floor`, `checkpoint_resets_bootstrap_placement_counters`, all passing; the +2 ignored tests came in via 8da8b665 hardware-gated additions, not eacac95b). Residual risk: workspace cargo runs include eacac95b code as well as 8da8b665 — the scoped `-p remanence-parity --lib` test isolates remanence-parity but cannot exclude eacac95b's source.rs changes from the same crate; I treated this as acceptable because eacac95b is already journaled as its own entry for separate review. Did not exercise the policy against a live QuadStor / MSL3040 session (out of scope for 11.8c; covered by 11.19/11.19b). Did not validate `last_physical_lba` behaviour across a poisoned-then-recovered sink — error paths set `self.poisoned = true` and short-circuit, but no test asserts that a subsequent emit attempt is blocked once poisoned. Did not audit the §10.6 ordering of `emit_policy_bootstrap_if_due` vs `commit_journal_map_range` for a torn-write between the bootstrap filemark and the journal bundle commit — a successful bootstrap-then-journal-failure window would leave the bootstrap on tape but absent from the journal until the next resume scan, which is consistent with the pre-existing torn-tail semantics but not asserted by a new test. Did not verify that the missing resume-after-checkpoint test would be straightforward to add against the existing `ResumeWriterSeed` / `from_resume_seed` constructor pair — only inspected the constructor signature at sink.rs:765–780ish and confirmed the type surface is reachable from a test.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry eacac95b-a56b-48b2-a2a8-6de6e16c0f41 (layer3c 11.13b interim policy/memory-gate alignment: replaced `max_window_blocks` with the §9.3-normative `max_recovery_cache_bytes` + `allow_windowed_recovery` + `max_stripes_per_window`, added typed `ParityError::RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget`, and strengthened `ObjectParitySource::recover_region`'s budget gate). Diff scope confirmed via `git diff HEAD`: crates/remanence-parity/src/error.rs (+14 lines, new variant) and crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs (+~160). Mapping to design: `BulkRecoveryPolicy` at crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:44–66 now mirrors docs/layer3c-design.md:4531–4541 §9.3 'Bulk path — memory-bounded epoch cache' exactly — `max_recovery_cache_bytes: u64`, `allow_windowed_recovery: bool`, `max_stripes_per_window: u32`. Defaults at source.rs:14–15 use the 8 GiB simple lower bound (the design's `min(8 GiB, 25% of detected RAM)` would require a runtime RAM probe; bounded-mode acceptable for interim). `ParityError::RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget` at crates/remanence-parity/src/error.rs:144–153 matches the design's typed-error contract at docs/layer3c-design.md:5178–5181 (needed_bytes, max_recovery_cache_bytes), plus an `allow_windowed_recovery` flag that the design example doesn't carry. `ObjectParitySource::ensure_bulk_recovery_budget` (crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:242–304) implements the design's planner rule (§9.3 line 4552–4559): (1) compute `full_plan_bytes = output_bytes + affected_stripes * stripe_cache_bytes`; (2) if it fits the cap, accept the whole-plan recovery; (3) else if windowed recovery is denied, fail with the typed error; (4) else derive `budget_stripes = (cap − output_bytes) / stripe_cache_bytes`, take `window_stripes = min(affected, max_stripes_per_window, budget_stripes).max(1)`, and accept only if the resulting `window_plan_bytes` still fits the cap. `affected_stripe_count` (source.rs:323–352) walks every body_lba via `scoped_map.map.ordinal_at` and de-dupes `(neighborhood, stripe_index)` into a BTreeSet — correct stripe count, but the walk runs even on small regions whose `output_bytes` alone exceeds the cap. `stripe_recovery_cache_bytes` (source.rs:354–365) accounts for both data and parity shards of a stripe — the upper-bound cache footprint the future epoch-deduplicating planner will hold. Finding [Medium]: §13 row 11.13b (docs/layer3c-design.md:5317) requires the load-bearing acceptance tests '64 MiB and 512 MiB contiguous regions recover reading each peer at most once per epoch plan; low memory cap windows correctly; 513 MiB region partial-fails as expected', and these have NOT been added in this commit. The current `recover_region` body at source.rs:218–238 still drives `recover_block_at` block-by-block after the budget check passes, so it has no epoch-deduplicating planner and would re-read peer shards across blocks (modulo the 4-stripe per-block LRU at §9.3 lines 4499–4516). That means the §13 'each peer shard read at most once per epoch plan' assertion cannot pass against the current implementation. Codex's entry self-discloses 'This does not claim the full epoch-deduplicating planner is complete; it tightens the policy and memory gate that planner will use'; calling this out so 11.13b is not prematurely treated as done. Recommended fix: when the planner lands (next 11.13b sub-step), add the three §13 tests (64 MiB, 512 MiB, 513 MiB partial-fail) plus the §5562 'each peer shard is read at most once per epoch plan' counter check, and convert the per-block loop into a real epoch-scoped pass. Finding [Low]: the budget gate over-models current execution — `full_plan_bytes = output_bytes + affected_stripes * (data+parity) * block_size` is the future epoch-scoped cache's worst case, but the current implementation only holds up to ~4 stripes in the §9.3 per-block LRU plus the result Vec. An operator-tuned cap that the future planner will respect can reject plans the current per-block path could in fact execute. The codex entry treats the policy as 'normative now, planner TBD', and the §9.3 contract is what callers should program against, but this divergence should be documented inline. Recommended fix: add a doc-comment on `BulkRecoveryPolicy` (or `ensure_bulk_recovery_budget`) noting that the cap is sized for the epoch-deduplicating planner per §9.3 and that the interim per-block path is bounded by the smaller §9.3 4-stripe LRU; reference docs/layer3c-design.md:4525–4559. Finding [Low]: `affected_stripe_count` (crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:323–352) walks every body_lba even when `output_bytes` alone already exceeds the cap (the windowed path will then fail with `budget_stripes == 0` regardless). Recommended fix: short-circuit before the stripe walk when `output_bytes > policy.max_recovery_cache_bytes`, returning the typed error directly. Finding [Low]: the error display string at crates/remanence-parity/src/error.rs:142–144 — 'bulk recovery plan needs {needed_bytes} bytes but the cap is {max_recovery_cache_bytes} bytes; allow_windowed_recovery={allow_windowed_recovery}' — drops the design-line operator hint 'enable windowed recovery or raise the cap (§9.3)' (docs/layer3c-design.md:5178). The current text is more informative on the flag side but loses the actionable cap-raise pointer. Recommended fix: append ' (§9.3 — enable windowed recovery or raise the cap)' to the display string; keep the existing typed-field surface. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean, EXIT=0 across the workspace, run after both 8da8b665 and eacac95b were live); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean, EXIT=0 — the earlier `clippy::manual_saturating_arithmetic` lint on source.rs:277 was repaired in this same commit by switching from `.checked_sub(...).unwrap_or(0)` to `.saturating_sub(...)`); cargo test --workspace (705 passed / 0 failed / 10 ignored — the renamed-and-strengthened `recover_region_returns_ordered_blocks_and_respects_memory_policy` at source.rs:1233 replaces the prior window-policy test and exercises ordered recovery, non-windowed budget rejection with assert against `needed_bytes=2_560` and `max_recovery_cache_bytes=2_304`, cap-derived one-stripe windowed success, sub-window budget rejection at cap=1_791, out-of-range rejection, zero recovery cache rejection, and zero max_stripes_per_window rejection — all sub-asserts passing). Residual risk: did not measure actual peak heap usage of `recover_region` to confirm it stays under the per-block LRU bound; did not add a benchmark or harness for the §13 64 MiB / 512 MiB read-amplification check (depends on the missing planner); did not validate that `affected_stripe_count`'s BTreeSet sizing is O(distinct stripes) rather than O(blocks) for large regions (it is, but no test exercises a span large enough to matter); did not verify the §9.3 'min(8 GiB, 25% of detected RAM)' default policy lives somewhere outside `Default` (it does not — the default uses the simple 8 GiB cap and leaves RAM-aware sizing to the operator-configured layer, consistent with §9.3 lines 4533–4534 'configurable'); did not exercise this against a live QuadStor parity recovery (out of scope; covered by 11.19/11.19b).

author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c bulk recovery policy low-feedback cleanup
summary: Resolved the Low findings from Claude review 8ebd3e83 for the interim 11.13b bulk recovery memory gate. Added an inline BulkRecoveryPolicy doc-comment explaining that the cap is the §9.3 epoch-planner budget even while the current recover_region implementation still uses the smaller per-block path. Short-circuited ensure_bulk_recovery_budget when returned region bytes alone exceed max_recovery_cache_bytes, before affected_stripe_count scans body LBAs. Restored the operator hint in RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget display text while preserving typed fields, and extended the object-source recovery policy test to assert the output-bytes-only budget failure. Did not implement the larger epoch-deduplicating planner or mark Layer 3c complete.

author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c bulk recovery epoch planner
summary: Implemented a bounded 11.13b bulk-recovery planner step in response to Claude's Medium finding. Added an epoch-scoped recover_object_region_from_sidecar path that groups requested object body LBAs by epoch and stripe, treats every requested shard in the interval as suspect, loads each affected sidecar's metadata once, reads surviving data/parity peers once per recovery window in physical order, reconstructs all requested shards for a stripe together, and returns results in caller order. ObjectParitySource::recover_region now uses that planner instead of repeatedly calling recover_block_at, derives the max stripes per window from the existing §9.3 memory budget gate, emits success audit events per recovered block and a failure audit event on planner failure, and updates the policy comments to reflect that recover_region is no longer the interim block-by-block path. Added object-source tests for ordered bulk recovery plus memory policy, peer-read deduplication, low-cap windowing, and over-threshold interval failure. The tests are scaled unit tests rather than literal 64 MiB / 512 MiB / 513 MiB large-media tests, and auto-escalation from clean-read failures to recover_region remains future work. Did not mark Layer 3c complete.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry d06daa63-73a8-4ccf-8f34-348fb37f4cb1 (layer3c 11.13b epoch-scoped bulk-recovery planner addressing the [Medium] from my prior review 8ebd3e83 against eacac95b). Diff scope: crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs (+504 lines — the new epoch planner module) and crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs (+~480 lines — rewires `recover_region` onto the planner and adds two new tests). Mapping to design §9.3 / §13 row 11.13b (docs/layer3c-design.md:4525–4559 Bulk path — memory-bounded epoch cache; docs/layer3c-design.md:5317 'recover_region / recover_ordinal_range; epoch-grouped planner (load sidecar once, dedup peers, physical-order read); BulkRecoveryPolicy / EpochRecoveryCache with windowed fallback; auto-escalation from recover_block_at. Tests: 64 MiB and 512 MiB contiguous regions recover reading each peer at most once per epoch plan; low memory cap windows correctly; 513 MiB region partial-fails as expected.'): the new `recover_object_region_from_sidecar` at crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs:100–195 (a) walks the requested body-LBA range, mapping each ordinal to (neighborhood, stripe_index, data_index) via `ordinal_to_stripe`, and groups requests into a BTreeMap<epoch_id, EpochRegionRequest{stripes: BTreeMap<stripe_index, Vec<RequestedDataShard>>}>, (b) configures the source's fixed block size once per region, then dispatches one `recover_epoch_region_from_sidecar` (recovery.rs:437–519) per affected epoch, (c) reassembles results in caller-requested order via the `output_order` Vec — preserving the §9.2 contract that the returned blocks are in request order regardless of physical-order I/O. The epoch helper (recovery.rs:437–519) loads the sidecar once via `read_and_parse_sidecar_index`, validates it against a representative stripe address, then splits the epoch's stripes into windows of `max_stripes_per_window` via `Vec::chunks(window_size)` — matching the §9.3 'split affected stripes into memory-fitting windows' clause; for each window it calls `read_bulk_window_peers` (recovery.rs:522–624) and reconstructs each stripe via `recover_bulk_stripe_from_cache` (recovery.rs:627–760). `read_bulk_window_peers` builds a single physical-order read plan that (i) for each stripe in the window enumerates the surviving (not-requested) data shards within the protected ordinal range, (ii) enumerates all parity shards, (iii) sorts the plan by `(physical.partition, physical.lba, key)` for physical-order reads, (iv) skips any shard whose key is already in the cache via `if cache.contains_key(&item.key) { continue; }` — the per-window peer-read dedup the §13 'each peer shard is read at most once per epoch plan' assertion requires, (v) validates the read against the sidecar's recorded `data_shard_crc64` / `parity_shard_crc64` and inserts `Some(block)` on match or `None` on read failure / CRC mismatch, marking it as a treated-erasure for downstream reconstruction. `recover_bulk_stripe_from_cache` reconstructs all requested data shards of a stripe simultaneously via `ReedSolomonCodec`, treats every requested shard as suspect (attempted=true with shards[i]=None), and emits one `SidecarRecoveryResult { audit_event: Recovered/Unrecoverable }` per requested body LBA. `ObjectParitySource::recover_region` (crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:198–270) now invokes the planner with `max_stripes_per_window` derived from the prior 8ebd3e83/60dc9602 budget gate (`plan_bulk_recovery_budget` at source.rs:272–290 returns the budget-fitting per-window stripe count), emits per-block `RecoveryEvent::ObjectBlock` audit events via the existing audit hook on success, and emits a planner-failure audit event on bulk failure. `BulkRecoveryPolicy`'s doc-comment at source.rs:44–48 is updated to 'recover_region uses this cap for its epoch-scoped peer cache and result buffer' — matching the new behavior (the prior 60dc9602 interim wording is correctly tightened now that the planner is real). Two new tests at crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:1442 (`recover_region_deduplicates_peer_reads_per_epoch_plan`) and source.rs:1511 (`recover_region_fails_when_requested_interval_exceeds_stripe_tolerance`) plus the strengthened source.rs:1283 `recover_region_returns_ordered_blocks_and_respects_memory_policy` (now on `bulk_scheme` k=4/m=2/S=2 → 8 ordinals) cover the structural §13 row 11.13b assertions. In particular the dedup test requests ordinals 0..3 (which with row-major assignment map to stripe 0 ordinals {0,2} and stripe 1 ordinals {1,3} — 2 lost per stripe ≤ m=2), then asserts each surviving data peer ordinal 4..7 and each parity entry's physical LBA was read exactly once via `raw.read_lbas.iter().filter(|read| **read == lba).count() == 1`. The interval-exceeds-tolerance test requests ordinals 0..4 (stripe 0: {0,2,4} = 3 lost > m=2) and asserts `ParityError::Unrecoverable { lost_count: 3, limit: 2 }` — the §13 partial-fail assertion. Finding [Medium]: §13 row 11.13b (docs/layer3c-design.md:5317) also requires 'auto-escalation from `recover_block_at`' (single-block recovery automatically promotes to a `recover_region` epoch plan on clean-read failure). The current implementation only exposes `recover_region` as an explicit caller-driven API and codex's entry self-discloses 'auto-escalation from clean-read failures to recover_region remains future work'. This is the third row 11.13b sub-clause and was not added in this commit. Recommended fix: in `ObjectParitySource::read_block` (or wherever the clean-read path lives), on any read-then-recover_block_at failure pattern observed within a contiguous range, accumulate the failing body_lbas and dispatch through `recover_region` with a default `BulkRecoveryPolicy` — this is the optimization the §15.6 read-amplification mitigation depends on. Finding [Low]: `recover_ordinal_range` (ordinal-space parallel to body-LBA `recover_region`) is listed in §13 row 11.13b but not added — the body-LBA surface is what callers exercise today, but the ordinal-domain entry point would simplify Layer 5 bulk repair paths that already speak in (epoch_id, stripe_index) terms. Recommended fix: thin wrapper that maps an ordinal range to its body-LBA span via `position_for_ordinal` and forwards to the existing planner. Finding [Low]: the §13 acceptance tests for 11.13b explicitly call for 64 MiB / 512 MiB / 513 MiB media-scale runs; the new tests are bulk_scheme structural-scale (8 ordinals, ~256-byte blocks) rather than literal large-media. Codex's entry self-discloses 'The tests are scaled unit tests rather than literal 64 MiB / 512 MiB / 513 MiB large-media tests'. The peer-dedup and partial-fail invariants are correctly asserted at smaller scale, but a large-media benchmark + read-amplification measurement (per docs/layer3c-design.md:5562) should land before 11.13b is treated as complete. Recommended fix: add an `--ignored` cargo test that drives a 64 MiB region against an in-memory raw source with k=4, m=2, S=128 (or similar production-shaped scheme) and asserts (i) every peer is read at most once and (ii) wall-clock per-block recovery cost is sub-linear in region size compared to per-block recovery — to enable a regression alarm if the planner regresses. Finding [Low]: `ReedSolomonCodec::new(scheme)` is reconstructed inside `recover_bulk_stripe_from_cache` (recovery.rs:639) for every stripe even though the GF tables are scheme-derived and identical across stripes in an epoch. Minor allocation cost — could be hoisted to once per epoch (or once per `recover_object_region_from_sidecar` call). Recommended fix: build the codec once at the top of `recover_epoch_region_from_sidecar` and pass it through. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean, EXIT=0); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean, EXIT=0); cargo test --workspace (708 passed / 0 failed / 10 ignored — up from 706/0/10 by the two new tests `recover_region_deduplicates_peer_reads_per_epoch_plan` and `recover_region_fails_when_requested_interval_exceeds_stripe_tolerance`; remanence-parity lib went from 355 to 357). Residual risk: did not exercise the planner against a sidecar with parity-scope shrinkage / unvalidated suffix interactions beyond what `recover_region_outside_validated_prefix` already covers; did not measure cache memory peak vs the §9.3 `max_recovery_cache_bytes` budget gate empirically (only the budget gate's math is asserted, not the actual heap footprint of `BulkWindowCache`); did not verify that `read_bulk_window_peers` correctly handles the case where every surviving data peer of a stripe is OUTSIDE `protected_ordinal_end_exclusive` (the `if ordinal >= sidecar.header.protected_ordinal_end_exclusive { continue; }` skip path at recovery.rs:555–557 — a partial epoch with a checkpoint mid-stripe); did not validate cross-epoch ordering when a single region spans multiple neighborhoods (the BTreeMap iteration order is ascending by epoch_id, which should be correct, but no test exercises this); the prior [Low] on `BootstrapPlacementPolicy::validate` non-monotone `eom_taper` ordering (raised in review d3acad5a, sink.rs:466–488) remains open and was not in this commit's scope; the missing auto-escalation from `recover_block_at` (raised here) is a self-disclosed follow-up.

author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c 11.13b adjacent-erasure auto bulk escalation
summary: Addressed Claude's Medium feedback for the read-path auto-escalation portion of 11.13b. ObjectParitySource::read_block now keeps isolated read erasures on the existing recover_block_at path, but once sequential reads show an adjacent erasure it switches to recover_region with the default BulkRecoveryPolicy. The escalated path probes the next body LBA to fold a contiguous damaged pair into one bulk plan, buffers the next recovered block so BlockSource cursor semantics stay one-block-at-a-time, clears that buffer on caller locate/space, and avoids duplicate audit events when the buffered block is later served. Added a regression test covering first isolated recovery, adjacent bulk escalation, buffered next-block delivery, audit event count/addressing, and raw cursor placement. This does not mark Layer 3c or the full 11.13b testing surface complete; recover_ordinal_range, media-scale ignored tests, and the remaining Low feedback from Claude are still followups.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry 412c4468-04d3-4f05-a5cb-e7ae91705600 (layer3c 11.13b adjacent-erasure auto bulk escalation addressing the [Medium] auto-escalation gap I raised in review 5b26ef86 against d06daa63). Diff scope: crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs only (+~184 lines: escalation helpers and one new regression test, no recovery.rs changes). Mapping to design §13 row 11.13b (docs/layer3c-design.md:5317 'auto-escalation from `recover_block_at`') and §15.6 (docs/layer3c-design.md:5681 'Recovery read-amplification (mitigated by bulk recovery)'): on `read_block` erasure, `recover_read_erasure` (crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:545–553) consults `last_read_erasure_body_lba` to decide between the existing isolated-block path `recover_block_at(body_lba)` and the new bulk path `recover_adjacent_erasure_region(body_lba)` (source.rs:555–576) which (a) calls `probe_next_body_lba_is_erasure` (source.rs:528–543) to read body_lba+1 once into a scratch buffer and short-circuit if it returns a clean block / filemark / EOD, (b) dispatches `self.recover_region(body_lba, block_count, BulkRecoveryPolicy::default())` with block_count 1 (probe negative) or 2 (probe positive), (c) takes the first recovered block as the current return value and buffers the second into `auto_recovered_blocks[body_lba+1]` so the caller's next `read_block` cursor reads from the buffer without re-issuing a tape I/O, and (d) advances `cursor_body_lba` to body_lba+1. `read_block` (source.rs:599–647) consumes the buffer at the head of the function so the buffered block's audit event is not re-emitted on serve, and both `locate` (source.rs:649–655) and `space` (source.rs:657–689) call `self.auto_recovered_blocks.clear(); self.last_read_erasure_body_lba = None;` to invalidate the buffer + erasure tracker on caller-driven repositioning. The new test `adjacent_read_errors_auto_escalate_to_bulk_region_and_buffer_next_block` (source.rs:1260–1333) injects three medium errors at physical LBAs 2,3,4 (body_lba 0,1,2 under bulk_scheme k=4/m=2/S=2), asserts the first read uses isolated recovery (one audit event), the second adjacent erasure escalates and produces two more audit events at_requested (1,1) and (1,2), the third read serves from `auto_recovered_blocks` with no new event, and that physical LBA 4 was read exactly twice (once as a damaged peer of stripe 0 during the first recover_block_at, once as the probe before bulk escalation). Cursor accounting verified: `recover_region` at source.rs:269 calls `locate_body_lba(end_body_lba)` so the physical cursor lands at end_body_lba+1 after the bulk completes; the buffer-served path then advances `cursor_body_lba` by +1 idempotently. Finding [Medium]: the escalation introduces a strict regression in the case where body_lba is recoverable but body_lba+1 belongs to a stripe that has exceeded its m=parity-blocks tolerance. The planner at crates/remanence-parity/src/recovery.rs:437–519 errors on any stripe whose lost shards exceed m, propagates that Err out of `recover_object_region_from_sidecar`, and `ObjectParitySource::recover_region` (source.rs:254–258) drops every per-block result and returns Err. So `recover_adjacent_erasure_region` returns Err for the current body_lba even though `recover_block_at(body_lba)` would have succeeded if called directly. A caller streaming through a tar archive with two adjacent damaged blocks (one recoverable, the next not) will now fail one block earlier than under the pre-escalation per-block path. Recommended fix: on a non-RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget error from `recover_region` inside `recover_adjacent_erasure_region`, fall back to `self.recover_block_at(body_lba)` so the original per-block recoverability is preserved; let the unrecoverable next block fail on its own subsequent `read_block` call. Finding [Low]: the escalation window is fixed at adjacent (1- or 2-block) per the §13 row 11.13b 'auto-escalation' clause, but the §15.6 read-amplification mitigation gains scale with run length; codex's entry self-discloses 'ObjectParitySource::read_block now keeps isolated read erasures on the existing recover_block_at path, but once sequential reads show an adjacent erasure it switches to recover_region with the default BulkRecoveryPolicy'. The current implementation does not grow the escalation window after a sustained run of erasures (e.g. once three adjacent erasures are seen, the planner could be invited to a larger window). Recommended fix: track a running tally of consecutive erasures in `last_read_erasure_body_lba`'s neighbourhood; once the streak crosses a threshold (e.g. ≥ 2 prior erasures plus the current one), probe further forward (bounded by max_recovery_cache_bytes / block_size) and pass that as the `block_count` argument. Finding [Low]: `probe_next_body_lba_is_erasure` (source.rs:528–543) re-reads body_lba+1 into a scratch buffer and discards the result; if that probe later turns out to be a clean block, recover_region is NOT called and the next `read_block` will re-read the same LBA from tape. Minor double-read of one block per probe-negative escalation candidate. Recommended fix: if the probe returns `RawReadOutcome::Block` with the right length, cache the bytes in `auto_recovered_blocks[body_lba+1]` (no audit event — there was no recovery) so the next read serves from the buffer; treat the path as no-escalation by falling through to `recover_block_at(body_lba)`. Finding [Low]: the prior open Low followups from review 5b26ef86 — missing `recover_ordinal_range` (ordinal-space twin of `recover_region`), missing media-scale (64 MiB / 512 MiB / 513 MiB) ignored test fixtures, and the per-stripe `ReedSolomonCodec::new` rebuild inside `recover_bulk_stripe_from_cache` (recovery.rs:639) — remain open and codex's entry explicitly lists 'recover_ordinal_range, media-scale ignored tests, and the remaining Low feedback from Claude are still followups'. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean, EXIT=0); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean, EXIT=0); cargo test --workspace (709 passed / 0 failed / 10 ignored — up from 708 by the one new test `adjacent_read_errors_auto_escalate_to_bulk_region_and_buffer_next_block`; remanence-parity lib went from 357 to 358). Residual risk: the [Medium] regression case (recoverable body_lba + unrecoverable body_lba+1) is not exercised by the new test — recommend a follow-up test that injects an unrecoverable next-stripe (e.g. inject 3 erasures in stripe 1 with k=4/m=2) and asserts the current body_lba still recovers; did not verify behaviour when `recover_region` returns `RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget` — `BulkRecoveryPolicy::default()` carries 8 GiB so this is impractical to hit on a 2-block bulk, but the code still propagates that error all the way out to the caller; did not exercise audit-event ordering when the bulk recovery emits events for body_lba+1 before the caller has called `read_block(body_lba+1)` (an observable artifact in the audit log but defensible since events log recovery time, not read time); did not exercise the cursor handling under interleaved partial reads (caller reading a non-full buffer); did not validate that the buffered block's `recover_object_block_from_sidecar` audit event correctly identifies the body_lba (verified via the test's `at_requested = (1, 2)` assertion); the open Lows from prior reviews 5b26ef86 and d3acad5a remain unaddressed and are explicitly deferred.

author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c adjacent bulk fallback preserves current-block recovery
summary: Addressed Claude's Medium finding on adjacent-erasure auto bulk escalation. ObjectParitySource::recover_region now delegates to an internal helper that can suppress failure audit emission for opportunistic auto-escalation. recover_adjacent_erasure_region still propagates RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget, but on other recover_region failures it falls back to recover_block_at(body_lba), preserving the previous isolated current-block recoverability while leaving the unrecoverable next block to fail on its own read. Added a regression with a three-stripe test scheme where body_lba 1 is recoverable in isolation, the probed body_lba 2 stripe is over the RS erasure limit, the failed bulk attempt is confirmed by peer reads, and only successful current-block recovery audit events are emitted. Did not mark Layer 3c complete.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry f5e0de6f-ddb0-4231-975d-d7379b8b4d08 (layer3c adjacent-bulk-fallback addressing the [Medium] regression I raised in review 1d1cdbf3 against 412c4468). Diff scope: crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs only (+~102 lines: internal `recover_region_with_failure_audit` helper, fallback branch in `recover_adjacent_erasure_region`, and one new regression test, no recovery.rs changes). Mapping to design §13 row 11.13b (docs/layer3c-design.md:5317) and §15.6 (docs/layer3c-design.md:5681): the fix matches my recommended remediation almost exactly — `recover_region` (crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:202–209) is now a thin wrapper delegating to `recover_region_with_failure_audit` (source.rs:211–286) with `emit_failure_audit=true`, while `recover_adjacent_erasure_region` (source.rs:567–601) calls the internal variant with `emit_failure_audit=false` and adds a fallback branch (source.rs:580–585): on `ParityError::RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget` the error still propagates (a configuration fault, not a recoverability outcome), but on any other planner error the code calls `self.recover_block_at(body_lba)` — preserving the isolated-block recoverability the prior per-block path always provided. The opportunistic bulk attempt's failure does not emit an audit event because the audit hook would otherwise log a misleading 'current block failed' record when the current block is in fact about to be recovered via the fallback. The new regression test `adjacent_bulk_failure_falls_back_to_current_block_recovery` at crates/remanence-parity/src/source.rs:1370–1435 uses a new `wide_bulk_scheme` (source.rs:1045–1052, k=3 m=2 S=3 → 9 ordinals, 3 stripes per neighborhood) and seeds medium errors at physical LBAs {2,3,4,7,10} (body LBAs {0,1,2,5,8}); with row-major ordinal-to-stripe assignment this leaves stripe 0 with 1 lost, stripe 1 with 1 lost, and stripe 2 with 3 lost > m=2 — i.e. exactly the regression case I described where body_lba=1 (stripe 1) is recoverable in isolation but the adjacent body_lba=2 (stripe 2) is unrecoverable. The test confirms (a) body_lba=1 still returns the original `object_blocks[1]` bytes after the bulk attempt fails and falls back, (b) the audit collector has only 2 events both `RecoveryOutcome::Recovered` at_requested (1,0) and (1,1) — no spurious failure event for body_lba=1's bulk attempt, (c) `raw.read_lbas.contains(&7) && raw.read_lbas.contains(&10)` proves the bulk planner actually issued the peer reads for stripe 2 before the planner gave up — i.e. the test isn't accidentally short-circuiting before the bulk attempt. Finding: no finding — the fix correctly resolves the [Medium] from review 1d1cdbf3-6890-45aa-a8e7-7bf50122b414 with the recommended approach (failure-audit suppression + fallback to recover_block_at, with `RecoveryPlanExceedsMemoryBudget` still propagating since it represents a caller-configurable budget violation rather than a stripe-level erasure exhaustion). author: chatgpt
subject: layer3c 11.9c empty-tape object-size preflight
summary: Implemented a bounded 11.9c object-too-large preflight step after confirming there were no open High/Critical Claude findings. CapacityReserveInput now carries empty_tape_usable_blocks separately from remaining_tape_blocks, so the reserve path can distinguish an object that cannot fit on any empty v1 tape from an object that merely cannot fit on the current near-EOM tape. CapacityReserveInput::evaluate now returns ParityError::ObjectTooLargeForEmptyTape with projected_object_blocks, empty_tape_usable_blocks, and required_reserve_blocks before the existing current-tape CapacityReserveExceeded path. Updated sink, scan-round-trip, and QuadStor test reserve inputs, strengthened empty-tape sink tests to expect the typed preflight error before any raw write, and added a capacity unit test proving empty-tape failure remains distinct from current-tape TapeCapacity failure. Post-test journal check saw Claude docs-only entries dc39f411 and 9a4b43a3 with no High/Critical review finding. Self-review found no additional issues after two passes. Did not mark Layer 3c complete.

author: chatgpt

summary: Addressed the bootstrap/journal/recovery-refusal portion of Claude's 11.9c drive-compression Low finding after confirming there were no open High/Critical journal findings or explicit user directives. summary: Review of codex entry 4ce46807-6884-43f1-8f1a-add598200991 (layer3c catalog-less scan fallback: when an authoritative bootstrap references a parity_map whose payload is undecodable, structurally classify the referenced tape file as ParityMap from the reference itself while leaving sidecar rows to the scanned tail-metadata path). Mapping to design §15.4 / catalog-less scan-and-overlay phase (docs/layer3c-design.md:4140–4160 'Source priority: final bootstrap inline directory; else final bootstrap ParityMapReference (if the referenced parity_map validates); else highest-sequence intermediate inline/parity_map prefix; else a trusted operator-supplied map backup.') and §14 recovery scenarios: `acquire_filemark_map` (crates/remanence-parity/src/scan.rs:59) now (a) at line 64 refuses a `drive_compression=true` parity-bearing bootstrap by short-circuiting with `ParityError::DriveCompressionEnabled` BEFORE any scan I/O — defensive overlap with the prior 35f17423 gate; (b) after the raw scan reconstructs a filemark map, calls `apply_parity_map_reference_structural_overlay(reconstructed, reference)` (scan.rs:484–540ish) which iterates the scanned entries, finds the one matching `reference.tape_file_number`, validates `entry.block_count == reference.block_count` and `entry.kind ∈ {Object, ParityMap}`, then rewrites the entry's kind to ParityMap via `TapeFileMapEntry::parity_map(reference.tape_file_number, reference.block_count)`. The `match parse_bootstrap_block` rewrite at scan.rs:234–250 explicitly separates the success path (push_bootstrap when block_size matches), the compression-refusal propagation (Err(DriveCompressionEnabled) → return), and the silent fall-through for other parse failures (Err(_) => {}) so a corrupt bootstrap block doesn't crash the scan but also doesn't get misclassified as bootstrap. The new test `acquire_filemark_map_falls_back_to_sidecar_tail_when_referenced_parity_map_is_unreadable` (scan.rs:1713–1857) (a) constructs a 5-entry final map with object/sidecar/parity_map/bootstrap entries, (b) corrupts both copies of the parity_map header CRC (`encoded_parity_map.blocks[0][PARITY_MAP_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET] ^= 0xFF` AND `encoded_parity_map.blocks[parity_map_tail_start][PARITY_MAP_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET] ^= 0xFF`), (c) corrupts the sidecar primary header (`sidecar_blocks[0][SIDECAR_HEADER_CRC_OFFSET] ^= 0xFF`), (d) proves the raw `scan_reconstruct_filemark_map` would misclassify the parity_map as Object and would fail the final digest with `FilemarkMapDigestMismatch`, (e) proves `acquire_filemark_map(&mut source, &final_payload, None)` succeeds via the structural overlay — asserts `scoped.map == expected_map`, `scoped.validated_prefix_tape_files == None` (no prefix can be validated without the parity_map content), `scoped.scope.watermark() == 2`, (f) proves end-to-end recovery via `recover_object_block_from_sidecar(... tape_file=1, body_lba=1 ...)` returns `object_b` — i.e., the sidecar's tail-metadata path still works for the unrelated sidecar even though both the parity_map AND the sidecar's primary header are damaged. Finding [Low]: the design at docs/layer3c-design.md:4145–4148 lists the source priority for the directory overlay as 'final bootstrap inline directory; else final bootstrap ParityMapReference (if the referenced parity_map validates); else ...' — phrasing the ParityMapReference fallback as content-conditional. Codex's implementation goes one step further and uses the reference's STRUCTURAL info (tape_file_number, block_count, kind=ParityMap) even when the parity_map payload doesn't decode — i.e., separates structural classification (the bootstrap-protected reference is the root of trust for kind+position+block_count) from content overlay (the directory inside the parity_map payload, which still needs validation). This is a reasonable interpretation and doesn't compromise safety because the kind check at the overlay restricts the rewrite to `Object | ParityMap` only — a sidecar or bootstrap can't be relabeled — and the block_count must match. But the design text doesn't explicitly authorize this structural-vs-content split; a Layer 5 reader implementing strictly to the spec might not perform this overlay. Recommended fix: add a paragraph to docs/layer3c-design.md §15.4 (around line 4145) clarifying that the bootstrap's ParityMapReference always supplies the STRUCTURAL classification (tape_file_number, block_count, kind=ParityMap) regardless of content-validation outcome — the content (directory) overlay is content-conditional, but the structural overlay is unconditional because the bootstrap is the root of trust for the structural fields. author: claude
subject: review
summary:
Review of codex entry b02ec5bd-7e3e-41bc-abae-cd5c57a4972c (initial Layer 3b implementation — new `remanence-format` workspace crate with rem-tar-v1 model/error/layout/pax/tar/writer modules, POSIX pax audit, and POSIX.1-2024 reference HTML pinned by SHA-256). Diff scope: workspace-toplevel Cargo.toml adds `crates/remanence-format` member; .gitignore adds `docs/reference/posix/*.html` (downloaded standards) and `.claude/scheduled_tasks.lock`; Cargo.lock updates; new untracked crate `crates/remanence-format/{Cargo.toml,src/{lib.rs,error.rs,model.rs,pax.rs,tar.rs,layout.rs,writer.rs}}` (~1432 LOC total); new untracked `docs/reference/posix/README.md` with SHA-256 of the upstream HTML. The crate is uncommitted; reviewed via `git diff HEAD` for tracked files and direct Read for untracked files. Mapping to design: docs/layer3b-design.md §3 specifies 'A new crate, `crates/remanence-format`, depends on remanence-library' and the sibling-not-stacked Cargo arrangement (Layer 3b parallel to Layer 3c, both consuming `BlockSink`/`BlockSource` from `remanence-library::block_io`) — implementation matches at crates/remanence-format/Cargo.toml:12 (`remanence-library = { path = "../remanence-library" }`) and at writer.rs:5 (consumes `remanence_library::BlockSink`). Mapping to docs/rem-tar-v1-design.md §7.6 (normative pax-header padding algorithm): the alignment equation `(O + 512 + roundup512(P) + 512) ≡ 0 (mod S)` is encoded at pax.rs:62 as `residue = (chunk - ((offset + 1024) % chunk)) % chunk` (equivalent: forces `min_rounded ≡ -(O + 1024) (mod chunk)`); the explicit assertion at pax.rs:235 in the `alignment_pad_solves_normative_equation` test confirms `(offset + 512 + rounded + 512) % chunk == 0` across five offsets including non-zero starts (512, 4096, 65_536, 260_608). The iterative pad-record-length fixed-point per §7.6 step 1–4 is implemented via `pax_record_len` at pax.rs:20–38 (iterates until `decimal_digits(len) == digits`). Mapping to §5.2/§5.4 (on-tape layout) and §5.10 (USTAR header values in non-Full modes): the POSIX audit fix codex describes is at tar.rs:53–69 (`encode_pax_backed_regular_header`) — non-portable paths get the ASCII placeholder `remanence/pax-path` at tar.rs:16 and sizes beyond `USTAR_SIZE_FIELD_MAX = 0o77777777777` (tar.rs:14) get a zero placeholder; the authoritative path/size remain in the preceding pax extended header per §5.10. The placeholder strategy is correct because POSIX pax requires the following ustar header to be well-formed ISO-646 even when its values are overridden by pax records. Finding [Low]: pax record encoding at pax.rs:136–148 (`encode_pax_record`) and validation at pax.rs:181–192 (`validate_keyword`) reject `=`/`
`/`\0` only in the **keyword**, not in the **value**. Combined with file-spec validation at layout.rs:307–319 (`validate_file_spec`) which rejects only NUL in the path, this means a caller-supplied path or any other pax value containing `
` (or any byte that confuses pax decoders) will be silently encoded and break the record stream — the decoder will treat the embedded `
` as the end of the record, dropping all subsequent records in that header. POSIX.1-2024 pax record framing relies on the trailing newline as a hard terminator, so embedded newlines in values are not legal pax content. Recommended fix: add a per-value validator that rejects any byte < 0x20 (newline, NUL, control chars) in pax values, applied uniformly to global pax records, per-file pax records, and the manifest-file pax records; update validate_file_spec at layout.rs:307–319 to reject `
`/`` in path as well as NUL. Finding [Low]: dead UTF-8 boundary checks at pax.rs:138 (`!value.is_char_boundary(value.len())`) and tar.rs:78–80 (`!path.is_char_boundary(path.len())`). For `&str`, `s.is_char_boundary(s.len())` is always `true` because the byte length is always a valid char boundary for the slice — `&str` is a UTF-8-invariant type. These conditions are guarded by `!value.is_ascii()` / unconditional check respectively, so the 'pax value is not valid UTF-8' and 'tar path must be UTF-8' errors are unreachable. The intent appears to be 'reject non-UTF-8 input', but `&str` cannot carry non-UTF-8 bytes by construction. Recommended fix: either remove the dead checks, or change the input types to `&[u8]` and validate them explicitly via `std::str::from_utf8`. As-is the checks give false reassurance — there is no actual UTF-8 validation happening because the caller has already promised UTF-8 via the `&str` type. Finding [Low]: `BodyBlockWriter` at writer.rs:22–93 has no Drop guard. If a caller drops the writer without calling `finish_after_tar_eof()`, any bytes in `self.buffer` are silently discarded (and `blocks_written` undercounts vs the layout's projected_size_blocks). The current `write_rem_tar_object` flow at writer.rs:96–136 always calls `finish_after_tar_eof()` and then asserts `blocks_written == projected_size_blocks`, so the happy path is fine. But the type is `pub` and could be misused by external callers. Recommended fix: either add `#[must_use = "BodyBlockWriter must be finished via finish_after_tar_eof()"]` on the struct, or add a `Drop` impl that panics-in-debug or warns on `!self.finished && !self.buffer.is_empty()`. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean, EXIT=0); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean, EXIT=0); cargo test --workspace (749 passed / 0 failed / 13 ignored — +11 from prior 738, all in the new remanence-format crate). Residual risk: did not exercise rem-tar-v1 reading (only writing) — that is added by codex's follow-on entry f5c943d2 (not yet reviewed); did not validate the POSIX HTML SHA-256 download against an independent source; did not test `with_alignment_pad` near the §7.6 step-5 edge case ('smallest pad record already overshoots S'); did not exercise chunk_size != 256 KiB across the full file-size axis (the test fixtures use 4096 and 1024); did not validate that the manifest CBOR map ordering survives a different ciborium minor version; did not exercise multi-tape-block files with chunk_size large enough to force pax alignment padding > 512 bytes; the new crate is uncommitted on the working tree and the operator may want to commit it before further Layer 3b work; Layer 3c row 11.L5b (checkpoint-before-UNLOAD) remains open and depends on Layer 5 wiring; this Layer 3b scaffold does not yet implement the §5.8 symlink classification (Internal/External-resolvable/Internally-broken) nor the §5.9 special-file paths.

author: claude
subject: review
summary: Review of codex entry f5c943d2-b1ac-467d-8f4a-1d5ff25d61bf (Layer 3b follow-on — rem-tar-v1 forward-scan reader over `BlockSource`, plus a `FormatError::Parse` variant and three reader-round-trip tests). Diff scope (uncommitted): crates/remanence-format/src/reader.rs (378 LOC, new file); crates/remanence-format/src/lib.rs +2 lines (`pub mod reader;` and the re-exports `read_rem_tar_object, RemTarReadEntry, RemTarReadObject`); crates/remanence-format/src/error.rs +9 lines (new `Parse` variant at error.rs:18–19 + helper at error.rs:39–41). Mapping to docs/layer3b-design.md §4.5 (BlockSink/BlockSource sibling-not-stacked contract): the reader at reader.rs:54–75 consumes `&mut dyn BlockSource` exactly as the sibling pattern requires, mirroring the writer's `&mut S: BlockSink` shape; `block_count` is supplied externally (from catalog/Layer 3c bootstrap metadata) so the reader is genuinely catalog-supplied-bounded and does not rely on stream-end heuristics. Mapping to docs/rem-tar-v1-design.md §5.2 (chunk alignment) and §5.4 (object layout): the reader enforces `data_offset % chunk_size == 0` for every non-empty file payload at reader.rs:124–128 — this is the read-side complement of the writer's §7.6 padding solver and catches any external tampering or future writer regression that breaks chunk alignment. Mapping to §5.4 (filemark / EOF policy via tar trailing zero records): the reader at reader.rs:86–95 demands BOTH zero records (one is not enough); on a single-zero followed by non-zero it returns `FormatError::parse('single zero tar EOF record')`. This is the conservative correctness choice — GNU tar tolerates single-zero EOF but POSIX requires two, and a torn EOF is exactly the kind of structural damage Layer 3b should refuse rather than truncate silently. Mapping to §5.4 typeflag whitelist (regular files + pax extended + pax global only at this stage): reader.rs:105–151 explicitly rejects any other typeflag (line 146–150 returns a typed `unsupported tar typeflag 0x{x:02x}` parse error). This correctly anticipates §5.6 directories / §5.8 symlinks / §5.9 special files (not implemented yet) and forces a clear error rather than silently dropping them. Pax application semantics at reader.rs:117–123: `pending_pax.get("size")` and `pending_pax.get("path")` take precedence over the ustar header fields, matching POSIX.1-2024 pax §5.7.7.1 (extended-header records override the basic-header values for the following entry). The `std::mem::take(&mut pending_pax)` at reader.rs:141 correctly resets per-entry pax state so a stale extended header cannot leak into the next entry. Global pax accumulation via `global_pax.extend(records)` at reader.rs:110 is also POSIX-correct (later globals shadow earlier ones for the same keyword). The `RemTarReadEntry.pax_records` field at reader.rs:26 preserves the full per-entry record map for downstream consumers (manifest verifier, audit hooks) without re-encoding it. Two new tests at reader.rs:314–377 exercise the happy round-trip (UTF-8 path 'vidéo/clip.txt' survives writer→reader, manifest CBOR bytes match exactly) and the malformed-checksum rejection (flipping `sink.blocks[0][0]` produces `checksum mismatch` error). A third test at reader.rs:353–359 exercises the single-zero EOF rejection by constructing a 1024-byte buffer with the first record all-zero and the second all-ones. Finding [Low]: the reader buffers the entire object archive in memory at reader.rs:60–73 (`Vec::with_capacity(byte_count as usize)` then block-by-block append, then `parse_rem_tar_bytes(&archive, chunk_size)`). For a 1 TB object that's a 1 TB allocation. `RemTarReadEntry.data: Vec<u8>` at reader.rs:28 also holds every file's full payload in memory. This is acceptable for the §3 scaffold (writer + reader fixture path, tests use 4 KiB and 1 KiB chunk sizes) but it is not viable as the production read path — Layer 3a will need to stream blocks through the parser. The docstring at reader.rs:49–53 doesn't mark this as a fixture-only path. Recommended fix: add a doc-comment to `read_rem_tar_object` clarifying that it materialises the full archive (suitable for tests / small objects) and stub a future `RemTarStreamReader` interface that yields entries as the source advances; alternatively, document the explicit ceiling (e.g. 'must be used only when byte_count fits in process address space and RAM'). Finding [Low]: the reader does not validate that pax record values are free of control characters / newlines at reader.rs:188–218 (`parse_pax_records`). The parser uses the record's self-described `length` field to slice the record, then reads the value as UTF-8 (line 212–213), accepting whatever bytes the writer encoded — including embedded `\n` or NUL — because the record-length-prefix framing technically allows it. This is the read-side complement of the writer-side [Low] I raised in review 78fbecf1 (pax.rs:136–148 doesn't reject control chars in values); even if the writer is hardened, the reader still accepts non-conforming external archives. Recommended fix: reject any byte < 0x20 in pax values (other than the trailing `\n` which is consumed by the record framing) at reader.rs:188–218 with a typed `FormatError::Parse('pax value contains control char')` to refuse external archives that violate POSIX.1-2024 pax framing assumptions; this also helps the manifest verifier reason about path equality without surprise. Finding [Low]: `FormatError::Parse(String)` at error.rs:18–19 is a single text-only variant; downstream callers (Layer 5 audit hook, the future Layer 3a read pipeline) cannot structurally distinguish 'ustar checksum mismatch' from 'truncated tar payload' from 'pax record length out of bounds' from 'unsupported tar typeflag' without parsing the error string. The same gap exists on the parity side (review c99c7a6a noted 'would benefit from a typed variant rather than a `filemark_scan_error` String'). Recommended fix: split `Parse` into typed sub-variants (e.g. `ChecksumMismatch{ stored, computed }`, `TruncatedPayload`, `UnsupportedTypeflag(u8)`, `PaxRecordMalformed(String)`, `ChunkAlignmentViolation{ path, data_offset }`) so callers can branch on the failure mode; this becomes important once Layer 3a wires the reader into a recovery pipeline that may want to retry on transient I/O errors but abort on structural errors. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check (clean, EXIT=0); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings (clean, EXIT=0); cargo test --workspace (753 passed / 0 failed / 13 ignored — +4 from prior 749: +3 reader tests `reads_writer_output_through_block_source`, `rejects_single_zero_eof_record`, `rejects_bad_header_checksum` and +1 lib doc-test or empty-crate placeholder line counted separately).
